V1.title: Prophet Goes S.Platt!!! Comic Book
V1.historicalContext: The image shows a comic book titled 'Prophet Goes S.Platt!!!'. The art style suggests it's likely from the late 20th or early 21st century, possibly the 1990s, given the emphasis on exaggerated musculature and detailed weaponry. The presence of the title 'Prophet' indicates that it is part of the Rob Liefeld's Extreme Studios imprint from Image Comics era.
V1.collectorSignificance: The comic book appears to be in good condition, potentially bagged and boarded. The title indicates it is either a rare issue or a special variant cover. The presence of 'S.Platt' in the title could refer to a specific artist or creator involved with the comic, potentially increasing its collectibility. Condition and rarity determine value.

V2.title: Prophet Goes S.Platt Comic Book
V2.historicalContext: Based on the visual style and title, this appears to be a comic book from the 1990s or early 2000s, likely from an independent publisher or a creator-owned series. The phrase 'GOES S.PLATT!!!' suggests a variant cover or special edition featuring art by a specific artist, possibly named S. Platt. The style is indicative of the Image Comics era, known for its dynamic artwork and independent creator ownership.
V2.collectorSignificance: The presence of the artist's name prominently on the cover suggests that this comic is likely a variant or special edition. Condition is very important for valuing comic books. The comic appears to be sleeved which offers some protection and may boost value if in excellent condition. The abundance suggests it isn't extremely rare but condition and S. Platt's reputation could impact collector interest.
V2.valuation: $5 - $20. Value will vary significantly based on condition, whether S. Platt is a significant artist, and if this is a known rare variant. Unopened condition could greatly increase value.

V3.title: Prophet Goes S.Platt!!! Comic Book
V3.historicalContext: Based on the style and title, this is likely a comic book from the 1990s or early 2000s, part of the "extreme" comic book aesthetic popular at the time. Rob Liefeld's work is often associated with this style.
V3.collectorSignificance: The comic book is in what appears to be good condition, sleeved in plastic. The presence of 'S.Platt' in the title could indicate a special edition featuring artwork or writing by Simon Platt. Further details (issue number, publisher) are needed for a definitive assessment. A signature by the artist would increase value.
V3.valuation: Without knowing the issue number or publisher, and assuming it's a regular edition, a fair auction estimate would be $5 - $15. If it is a special edition or contains a signature by a well-known artist (e.g., Simon Platt or Rob Liefeld), the value could be significantly higher, potentially $20 - $50 or more, especially if graded.

V4.title: Prophet Goes S.Platt!!! Comic Book
V4.historicalContext: The image shows a comic book titled 'Prophet Goes S.Platt!!!'. Given the artistic style, it is likely from the late 20th or early 21st century, a period known for dynamic comic book art. The comic appears to be part of a larger collection.
V4.collectorSignificance: The comic book appears to be in good condition, potentially graded. S. Platt likely refers to the artist or writer involved in the book. The value is contingent on the popularity of the Prophet series and the significance of the particular issue. The '!!!' suggests that this comic is a unique or variant cover.
V4.valuation: $10 - $50, dependent on condition, rarity, and demand. If this is a rare variant cover or signed by a significant creator, the value could be significantly higher. Check for confirmed signature.

V1.title: Denver and Rio Grande Railway Freight Bill, 1883
V1.cleanedTranscription:
FORM 200.-0-10-82-100M
FREIGHT AGENT'S OFFICE,
No.
Denver and Rio Grande Railway.
Received from Hett, Fouchkune to D.D. Belden
in apparently good order, (contents and value unknown,) consigned to 
State of Cole
Seby 5 1883
the following described property.
Red Cliff marked as noted in margin, to be
transported over the line of the Railway to the Company's Freight Station at and delivered in like good
order to the consignee or owner, AT SAID STATION, or to such Company or Carrier (if the property is to be forwarded beyond said
Station') whose line may be considered a part of the route to the place of destination of said goods or packages; it being distinctly
understood that the responsibility of this Company as a common carrier shall cease at the Station where delivered to such Company
or carrier; but it guarantees that the rate of freight for the transportation of said property from the place of shipment to the place
swhere consigned, shall not exceed per and charges advanced by this Company,
UPON THE FOLLOWING CONDITIONS:
This Receipt to be presented without Alteration or Erasure.
AS CONSIGNED MARKS. DESCRIPTION OF ARTICLES.
D.D. Belden 15 Bof Candles
R Cliff
Cols

Rates.
Special per
Amount Charges Advanced, $

Agent
V1.historicalContext: This document is a freight bill from the Denver and Rio Grande Railway, dated Sibley 5, 1883. It documents the receipt of goods from 'Hett, Fouchkune' consigned to 'D.D. Belden' to 'Red Cliff'. The item shipped is 'Bof Candles'. The Denver and Rio Grande Railway was a prominent railroad company in the late 19th century, crucial for connecting the mountainous regions of Colorado and providing transport for goods and passengers. The printed form indicates mass production of these forms, a standard practice for railway companies of the era.
V1.collectorSignificance: The document's significance lies in its representation of late 19th-century commerce and transportation. Its condition appears to be fair; the paper shows signs of age. The presence of a handwritten agent signature enhances its value. The form number '200.-0-10-82-100M' could potentially lead to discovering production run information which might affect the rarity. The watermarks if present would also contribute to its significance and authentication.
V1.valuation: $25 - $50. Due to the historical significance of the Denver and Rio Grande Railway and the relative availability of such documents, the value is moderate. A well-preserved example with clear signatures would be at the higher end of the range.

V1.title: Delta Mercantile Co. Letter, September 10, 1890
V1.cleanedTranscription:
THE DELTA MERCANTILE CO.,
(Successors to MOGBANAHAN & BUTLER,)
-DEALERS IN-
General Merchandise.

GEO. STEPHAN, Prest.
A. C. BUTLER, Vice-Prest.
FRANKE. DODGE, Sec'y and Treas.

Delta, Colo.,. Sep 10 1890

Mr. R. G. Comfrex
Shuory Cow

Gentlemen.

Can you give us
a Special rate on Laundry
Proper Shiped from this People.
to Arvon Laundries & return
What rate Can you give us

Awaiting Your Earliest Convenience
We remain your Props

THE DELTA MERCANTILE CO
Per: Stephan
V1.historicalContext: This is a letter from The Delta Mercantile Co. of Delta, Colorado, dated September 10, 1890. The letter is addressed to Mr. R.G. Comfrex of Shuory Cow, presumably another business. It inquires about special rates for shipping laundry, possibly indicating The Delta Mercantile Co. was involved in supplying goods or services related to laundry businesses in the region. The letterhead identifies Geo. Stephan as President, A.C. Butler as Vice-President, and Frank E. Dodge as Secretary and Treasurer. It also notes the company's status as successors to Mogbanahan & Butler. The letter provides a glimpse into business correspondence and supply chains in late 19th-century Colorado.
V1.collectorSignificance: This letter is significant due to its age, origin from a specific company in a specific location (Delta, Colorado), and the presence of signatures (possibly autopen) from company officers. The condition of the paper (presumably fragile) impacts its value. The unique typography and the letterhead's information about the company's lineage as successors to Mogbanahan & Butler contribute to its historical interest. The content regarding laundry services provides a unique insight into the business landscape of the time.
V1.valuation: $35 - $75. The value is based on the age of the document (1890), its connection to a specific place and business (Delta Mercantile Co. in Delta, Colorado), and the presence of signatures. The subject matter (laundry rates) is relatively mundane, which somewhat limits the appeal. The condition, if fragile, could also slightly decrease the value.

The Delta Mercantile Co. identifies itself as successors to McGrahanan & Butler and deals in 'General Merchandise'. The letterhead identifies Geo. Stephan as President, A. C. V3.collectorSignificance: This is a nice example of late 19th-century business correspondence. The presence of a printed letterhead with named officers, combined with the handwritten text, is typical for the era. The subject matter relates to shipping rates, giving insight into the commercial landscape of the time. Condition will play a role in value. An intact, clean copy will be worth more. The relative obscurity of The Delta Mercantile Co. makes it somewhat less desirable than if it were associated with a major national company. However, letters relating to the laundry and shipping industry from this era are of interest to specialists.
V3.valuation: $25 - $50. The letter has some collector interest due to its age, origin, and the identifiable company and officers. The specific rate query regarding laundry and shipping makes it a niche interest. The value is relatively modest because the company itself is not particularly famous.

V1.title: Denver and Rio Grande Railroad Company Letter, 1890
V1.cleanedTranscription:
2201

THE DENVER AND RIO GRANDE RAILROAD CO.

EXPRESS DEPARTMENT.

Denver, Colorado, Sept. 15th 1890

D. D. MAYO, GENERAL AGENT,
1609 LAWRENCE STREET.

Mr. M. M. Hastings
Agent Delta
Dear Sir
Please notify Mr. Stephan that
our rate on laundry matter, is
"pound rate" or 3/4c per pound No
charge less than 75c. Same must
be used to show what it is

Yours truly
D. D. Mayo

identifiedNames.name: Denver and Rio Grande Railroad Co.

V1.historicalContext: This is a letter from the Denver and Rio Grande Railroad Company, Express Department, dated September 15th, 1890. The letter informs Mr. M. M. Hastings, Agent Delta, about the rate for laundry matter: 3/4 of a cent per pound, with a minimum charge of 75 cents. The letter mentions that it should be used to show what it is, suggesting the letter serves as documentation or authorization of these shipping rates. D. D. Mayo signs the letter.
V1.collectorSignificance: This letter is significant for a few reasons. It provides a snapshot of the business operations of a major railroad company in the late 19th century. The original company letterhead with the vignette logo, the date and content is valuable, particularly if the signatures are authentic. Also, the physical condition contributes to the overall value. The printing quality is important as well.
V1.valuation: $50 - $150. The letter is historical and from a well-known railroad. The value is affected by the condition, presence of the original company letterhead, and the signature's authenticity. Expert appraisal would be needed for higher confidence.

Railroadiana is a popular collecting area, and original documents such as this command a premium.
